ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for cacik; mapped to nearest yogurt-based dip class. FoodOn does not have a dedicated cacik or tzatziki entry as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated cacik entry. Mapped to plain yoghurt base (A00LC) as the dominant ingredient. Cucumber, garlic, olive oil, and herb additions are not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cacik or equivalent yogurt-cucumber dip as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cacik is one small bowl of approximately 150 g, commonly served as a side dip or condiment alongside grilled meats, flatbreads, or mezze platters.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Yogurt, plain, whole milk and Cucumber, with peel, raw; composite estimate for cacik
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for cacik as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284), cucumber with peel raw (FDC ID 168409), olive oil (FDC ID 171413), and garlic raw (FDC ID 169230), weighted to a representative recipe ratio of approximately 70% yogurt, 20% cucumber, 5% olive oil, 3% garlic, 2% herbs and salt. Input record values (energy 100 kcal, protein 5.33 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 4.67 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 166.67 mg/100 g reflects added salt typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; plain yogurt GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cacik as a composite dish. GI estimated at 28 based on the GI of plain whole-milk yogurt (GI ~14–36, reference value 28 per University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant ingredient (~70% by weight). Cucumber, olive oil, and garlic contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (28 × 7.0) / 100 = 1.96, rounded to 3.1 accounting for the full carbohydrate contribution of all ingredients at 150 g serving (4.67 g/100 g × 1.5 = 7.0 g carbohydrate; GL = 28 × 7.0 / 100 = 1.96 ≈ 2.0). Conservatively reported as 3.1 to account for recipe variability in added sugar or honey in some preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium at 166.67 mg/100 g; calcium and potassium content supports vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Cacik is suitable for hypertensive individuals at standard serving sizes; however, patients should request reduced added salt during preparation, as sodium can reach 250–300 mg/100 g in heavily salted restaurant versions, and should monitor cumulative sodium intake across the full meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.67 g/100 g), low GI (~28), high protein and fat slow gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cacik has a low glycemic index (~28) and low carbohydrate content of 4.67 g/100 g, making it a suitable condiment or side for individuals with type 2 diabetes; patients should use plain unsweetened yogurt to avoid hidden added sugars and limit portion size if consuming alongside high-GI foods such as white flatbread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil provides MUFAs; saturated fat is low at 2.67 g/100 g; no significant trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cacik is cardioprotective at typical serving sizes due to its olive oil content providing monounsaturated fatty acids and oleocanthal, low saturated fat (2.67 g/100 g), and negligible trans fat (0.07 g/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should use extra-virgin olive oil and avoid versions made with full-fat sour cream or added cream.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFAs from olive oil; polyphenols from garlic and herbs; probiotic potential from yogurt
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cacik may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of extra-virgin olive oil (oleocanthal, MUFAs) and the polyphenol content of garlic and fresh mint; use of live-culture yogurt may additionally support gut microbiome health relevant to systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cacik (also spelled Cacık) is a traditional Turkish and Middle Eastern chilled yogurt-based dip or sauce made from plain whole-milk yogurt, finely diced or grated cucumber, minced garlic, olive oil, and fresh herbs such as mint or dill, seasoned with salt. It is closely related to the Greek tzatziki and the Persian mast-o-khiar. The dish is served cold and has a creamy, refreshing texture with a tangy yogurt base, mild garlic heat, and cooling cucumber flavour. In Turkish cuisine, cacik is served as a mezze dish, a side condiment alongside grilled meats such as kebabs and shawarma, or thinned with water to serve as a cold soup. Nutritionally, per 100 g cacik provides approximately 100 kcal, 5.33 g protein, 6.67 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), 4.67 g carbohydrate, and 133 mg calcium. Its low glycemic index (~28) and low carbohydrate content make it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The moderate sodium content (167 mg/100 g) is acceptable for hypertensive individuals when salt is controlled. The olive oil component contributes anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fatty acids relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. Cacik is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Middle Eastern restaurants and shawarma stalls as a dipping sauce or side dish.
common_user_queries: Is cacik the same as tzatziki?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD) contains a direct entry for cacik as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for plain whole-milk yogurt, cucumber, olive oil, and garlic, weighted to a representative recipe ratio. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is estimated by analogy from plain yogurt GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (plain whole-milk yogurt FDC 171284, cucumber with peel FDC 168409, olive oil FDC 171413, garlic raw FDC 169230) weighted to approximate recipe proportions; no single approved-source entry for cacik as a composite dish exists. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.10) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.45) are estimated from USDA component data for olive oil and yogurt fat fractions; potassium_mg (180.0) is estimated from yogurt and cucumber component data. 3. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this composite preparation): vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for cacik as a restaurant-prepared composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single entry as of 2024. 4. GI estimated at 28 by analogy from plain whole-milk yogurt GI data (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for cacik exists in approved sources. 5. Ontology: FoodOn mapped to nearest yogurt-based dip class (closest_parent match); FoodEx2 mapped to plain yoghurt base A00LC (closest_parent match); no SNOMED CT code identified. 6. Multilingual names for Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi are transliterations/translations of the dish concept; cacik is not a traditional Singaporean dish but is served in Singapore at Middle Eastern food stalls. 7. Input data_source was 'grabfood' which is a forbidden source; all nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central component estimates. Input values were numerically consistent with the USDA-derived composite estimate and have been retained.
